case_name: Bond   v Specsavers International Healthcare Ltd & Anor [2021] EWHC 2525 (QB) (28 May 2021)

evidence_index.text: The claimant, Mr Jonathan Bond, was born on 25 March 1996. On 2 September 2012, when 16 years old, Mr Bond attended a branch of the first defendant, Specsavers in Accrington for an appointment.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr Bond described getting headaches in 2008 to 2009. He was referred to hospital for this and other potentially unrelated problems.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Expert evidence of optometry was provided by Ms Gillian Whitby, instructed on behalf of Mr Bond, and Mr Ian Cameron, instructed on behalf of Mr Moustafa.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In circumstances where I conclude on balance that further investigation was not undertaken by Mr Moustafa, it follows from that agreed position (which I accept) that Mr Moustafa should have referred Mr Bond.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Mr Bond's evidence was that his headaches continued on a regular basis.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr Bond's evidence was that initially, Specsavers told his mother that his previous eye test was done in October 2011 and therefore, he was not due an eye test until October 2012.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr Bond's evidence was that he explained to Mr Moustafa that he had been having difficult with his eyesight during his revision and exams and over the summer and had been having lots of bad headaches.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: A further appointment with Specsavers Accrington on 21 March 2013 followed. This was not with Mr Moustafa.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: His referral to hospital was to the Royal Preston Hospital on 4 April 2013.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The experts differed on their opinion as to the progression of the keratoconus, including acceleration of progression in this case.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: I am persuaded that on the balance of probabilities, had Mr Bond been referred in September 2012, cross-linking treatment would have been offered to him by November 2012.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The balance of probabilities is that he then would have avoided corneal transplant surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Mr. Bond reported worsening vision and frequent headaches during his GCSE revision period.

focused_exam.eyes: Large change in astigmatism in the left eye since previous appointment

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Vision problems with possible underlying pathology

reveal.result_summary: New glasses prescribed, large change in astigmatism noted
reveal.clinical_significance: Significant change in astigmatism should prompt further investigation
reveal.decision_impact: Referral to ophthalmology should have been considered

timeline_phases.name: Initial Optometry Visit
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to optometrist with vision issues

timeline_phases.name: Subsequent Hospital Referral
timeline_phases.description: Patient referred to hospital for further evaluation

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient presents with significant change in astigmatism
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in management?

options.description: Refer to ophthalmology for further evaluation
options.clinical_reasoning: Significant change in astigmatism suggests possible keratoconus, requiring specialist evaluation

options.description: Prescribe new glasses and schedule routine follow-up
options.clinical_reasoning: Failed to address potential underlying pathology

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant prescribed new glasses and set a 24-month follow-up
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant did not recognize the need for further investigation

expected_action_court.description: Referral to ophthalmology should have been made
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence indicated referral was necessary

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failed to refer despite significant astigmatism change
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Referral to ophthalmology for potential keratoconus

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to refer for further investigation
Causation established - earlier referral would have prevented severe outcomes

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to refer for further investigation
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Referral would have led to timely treatment

factual_timeline.event: Patient referred to Royal Preston Hospital

allegations.allegation: Failure to refer for further investigation
